entry.title: 探索SAP S/4HANA Cloud中的碳足迹功能
entry.content:
在快速变化的商业环境中，可持续发展已成为企业的重要战略。面对气候变化等问题，企业越来越需要采取行动，减少自己的环境影响。碳足迹，作为衡量企业温室气体排放的指标，成为关键关注点。

通过与SFM（SAP Sustainability Management）集成，SAP S/4HANA Cloud不仅帮助企业优化运营，而且能够将碳足迹分析直接应用于商业决策中。这样，企业可以详细了解自身的碳排放情况，并将这些数据有效地融入到决策过程，制定更环保和可持续的策略。

在这篇博文中，我们将深入探讨SAP S/4HANA Cloud中的碳足迹功能如何通过与SFM的集成，帮助企业在业务决策中实现可持续发展目标。

  • 介绍SFM

首先简单介绍SAP Sustainability Footprint Management（SFM）。SFM旨在跨温室气体 (GHG) 范围 1、2 和 3 计算和管理碳流程的解决方案。通过与ERP系统的集成，SFM能够实现准确高效的排放计算。这是从平均值向实际值转变、迈向交易性碳会计的第一步。SFM向企业提供其碳足迹的全面视图，使企业能够记录、报告并采取行动以脱碳其价值链。

更多有关于SFM的基本详细介绍，可以查阅Blog。

SAP可持续足迹管理（SFM）的一个大优势在于它能把企业的碳足迹计算融入到各个业务流程中。这种整合不仅带来了清晰的透明度，还为业务用户提供了关于可持续性的深入见解。这样一来，用户可以在决策过程中考虑可持续发展标准。通过这种方式，企业不仅能追踪和报告自己的可持续发展表现，还能在日常运营中主动采取措施，减少对环境的影响。

了解完SFM，接下来我们一起来探索SAP S/4HANA Cloud中有哪些碳足迹功能。

 

  • 查看产品足迹

在SAP S/4HANA Cloud中，企业可以使用“查看产品碳足迹”的应用程序来查看由SAP可持续足迹管理（SFM）计算出的产品足迹。该应用为用户提供了直观且高效的方式来访问和管理产品的碳足迹数据。

每个产品足迹都由特定的参数组合来确定，比如产品和工厂或产品、工厂及供应商，再加上计算期间、足迹类别（例如温室气体）和参考基准（例如每单位平均足迹）。产品足迹数据是有时间依赖性的，其有效期会根据计算期间自动确定。这意味着用户能够查看不同时段和不同条件下的碳足迹数据，为环境影响分析提供更全面的信息。

 

 

  • 采购

在采购环节，SAP S/4HANA Cloud实现了产品碳足迹与采购申请（PR）和采购订单（PO）的直接集成。这是SAP S/4HANA Cloud在采购流程中的重要的集成场景。通过这一功能，采购申请和采购订单中的每个项目都能显示出碳足迹信息，使得审批人和业务人员不仅可以看到采购申请的财务价值，还能了解其环境影响。

 

 

同时，在“监控采购申请项目”应用程序中，采购人员也可以分析他们负责的采购申请所带来的环境影响。

 

这个应用程序不仅显示已完成的采购，还展示了那些预期未来交付的采购申请，这为采购人员提供了预期排放的重要信息。通过利用这些信息，企业可以采取积极的措施来优化采购流程的环境足迹。

 

  • 库存管理

另一个集成是在库存管理中实现，首先是在“库存 - 多物料”应用程序中。这一改进主要是为库存管理员提供了库存对环境影响的洞见。以前，这个应用程序主要用于获取库存数量及其相应的财务价值概览。现在，它还能将可持续性标准纳入决策过程中。

 

这个应用程序从集成组件中提取碳足迹数据，并通过将每种物料的碳足迹乘以其数量，展示每种物料的总碳足迹。这种功能使库存管理员不仅可以根据数量和财务价值管理库存，还能够考虑其环境影响，做出更加环保的决策。

未来，SAP计划将这种集成扩展到更多的库存管理流程和应用程序中。

 

  • 关键用户可扩展性

将发布的碳足迹数据集成到业务流程中还可以利用关键用户可扩展性功能。关键用户可扩展性与SAP产品足迹管理中计算的产品足迹相结合，使客户能够将可持续性信息丰富到端到端的业务流程中，帮助企业实现绿色可持续发展。

例如，可以通过应用“自定义字段”和“自定义逻辑”，将碳足迹的信息补充到应用“管理采购订单”中的行项目中。

 

 

目前SAP S/4HANA Cloud中已有非常多关于可持续的CDS视图可供客户使用，具体可以查看链接，支持客户将碳足迹的信息融入更多业务场景中。

 

  • 总结

本博文主要探讨了SAP S/4HANA Cloud中的碳足迹功能，展示了如何利用这些功能帮助企业在业务决策中实现可持续发展目标。整合碳足迹管理到企业的采购、库存管理等关键环节，并利用关键用户可扩展性，企业可以更有效地降低碳排放，推动绿色经营策略。希望这篇博文能够为您的企业在实现可持续发展目标的道路上提供实用的洞见和参考。

entry.title: Transform Your GenAI-Based BTP Experience: Digital Assistant with Data Learning & Adaptive Prompting
entry.content:
In this blog, we will discuss how to use BTP services to create a personalized digital assistant that will help you conduct your business objectives. The blog can help in improving the digital assistant's ability to learn from current data. By separating the AI prompt from the main application, we have also enabled flexible prompting. This allows us to easily change the prompt and prevents the need to repeatedly deploy the application.

Our Business Context: The customer, utilizing S4 HANA public cloud, has relied on an external application for end-user timesheet submissions. Given the critical role of timesheets in their revenue process, simplifying the submission method was a significant challenge. This led us to develop a GenAI-powered digital assistant for the customer. This assistant can analyze historical entries, provide timesheet suggestions, and confirm user responses, thereby streamlining the submission process. With the GenAI-based assistant, users will spend significantly less time on timesheet submissions.

Pre-Read: I'll make an effort to provide all of the references we used to create this digital assistant, along with information about how they relate to one another. Here are things to consider before we get into the specifics of each step.

  1. Before activating, please check your BTPEA/CPEA credits as there are few paid services you must subscribe to. The consumption cost of AI models is covered in the last section I added.
  2. Select the AI model that you wish to employ. Below is a detailed explanation of the steps.
  3. Currently, the SAP BTP GenAI service doesn’t support deep learning capacity. However, in this instance, we tried a different strategy and succeeded in allowing the AI to gain knowledge from the historical data. However, the learning will only take place during one session. That is, the learning will be lost when we reload the application.
  4. To put the solution into practice, a business consultant and a CAP developer are needed.

So, let’s get started.

The architecture of the genAI-enabled digital assistant is shown below. The SAP documentation page below has further information on this. https://github.com/SAP-samples/btp-cap-genai-rag/blob/main/docs/architecture/multitenant-architecture.png

Fig 1: GenAI Architecture

Here, we developed an application for tracking and submitting timesheets. You may use business requirements to guide the design of your application. Let's go on to the steps.

  1. Subaccount setup. I won't go into specifics about how to set up a subaccount. The genAI services (AI Core, AI Launchpad) are not accessible in every region or from every provider. Please use SAP Discovery Center to check for availability (https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/estimator/?commercialModel=btpea). If you have a subaccount set up currently and AI services aren't supported there, please make a new subaccount just for AI services. You can still work on the application in your original subaccount. Since AI services are not supported by our development subaccount, this blog discusses the dual-subaccount configuration. 

     

  2. Services to be Procured. These are the services that need to be purchased. To assist you in activating or configuring each service, I have included remarks next to it.

    Service / Subscription

    Price

    Remarks

    SAP AI Core - Generative AI hub

    Paid

    Select “Standard” or “Extended” option.

    SAP AI Launchpad

    Free

    Free Tier

    SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtime

    Paid

    Available by default with CF

    SAP Cloud Management Service

    Free

    Activate if not there

    SAP Credential Store

    Paid

    Should be already there. If not, activate

    SAP HANA Cloud 

    Free

    Should be already there, if not activate the free one

    Each service may have a different price; however you can check the SAP Discovery Center for pricing information by using this link. https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/estimator/?commercialModel=btpea

     

  3. HANA DB setup
    1. For Context:  Each conversation's "context" is stored in HANA DB. We must also pass the historical conversation to genAI for every message received by the digital assistant. A single table contains all of this history.
    2. Flexible Prompting: In order to give the program flexible prompting, we also use a different table to contain the prompting text. The CAP application is related to this. By doing this, we can make sure that any changes to the prompt don't need redeploying the entire program.

       

  4. SAP AI Core Service: The most important phase in the development of this application is this one. The sub-steps to be taken are listed below.
    1. Activate the service “SAP AI Core”.
    2. Go to subaccount->Instances and Subscription-> Instances -> Open the Sap AI core service.
    3. Click on “Create Service Keys” to create new key for connection.
    4. Click on the generated key and pick the values of parameters “clientid” and “clientsecret”. Keep these settings handy since you'll need them when you use destination to link the application to AI Core.
    5. AI Launchpad setup – Configuration: Subscribe to the service “SAP AI Launchpad”.
    6. Select ML operations, Configurations and then Create configurationTraverse to subaccount->Instances and Subscription-> Subscriptions -> select “SAP AI Launchpad” and open the application.
    7. Select ML operations, Configurations and then Create configuration.
    8. You must enter a configuration name and fill in the properties as seen below in the create configuration screen (this is what I used, but you can modify it to suit your needs).
    9. In the next screen, give a model name and version. You can either chose default (which is what we did) or select other models. Currently supported models are gpt-35-turbo, gpt-4o, gpt-4, gpt-35-turbo-0125, gpt-35-turbo-16k, gpt-4-32k, gpt-4o-mini, text-embedding-ada-002, text-embedding-3-small, text-embedding-3-large.
    10. You don’t need to select any input parameters. Click on next, review and save. Now, the configuration is created.
    11. You are free to decide the model you wish to use.
    12. Deployment > take URL. 

       

  5. AI Launchpad setup – Deployment: Once the configuration is created, the next step is to create a deployment for the config. To do this, below are the steps:
    1. Open the created configuration (SAP AI Launchpad -> Configurations).
    2. Click on create deployment.
    3. Select the duration till when the deployment needs to be active, review and save.
    4. Scroll to “Deployments” section, select the currently created deployment and copy the URL until …ondemand.com. Note down this URL. 
    5. Destinations setup (to AI and to backend): After setting up all the configurations, we must build destinations to link the application to the backend and AI to the application. The subaccount where you plan to deploy your application (where Business Application Studio is activated) must have all these destinations created.
    6. Connect to AI Core and Launchpad.
      • Get the URL from the SAP launchpad deploy step (step 6.d of the blog) https://api.ai.prod.us-east-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com/
      • Authentication: “OAuth2ClientCredentials”.
      • Set client Id and client secret values from step 4.d.
      • Give the subaccount’s token service URL. This needs to be the token service URL of the subaccount which has the AI Core activated.
    7. To the backend: Create a destination like how you normally would do for any backend connection.

       

  6. Design directions for Source code development: Please be aware that this is not an extract of the entire source code. You may find instructions on how to use the code included in the SAP GitHub link here. https://github.com/SAP-samples/btp-ai-core-simplifying-timesheet-tracking-task-in-s-4hana-cloud?files=1

    Prompt Design and Enabling Data Analysis

    In this situation, we must allow the AI Core to function as an assistant capable of carrying out the following tasks.

    1. Engage in productive user interaction.
    2. Examine data and produce insights that people can benefit from.
    3. Develop an API payload that can be used to upload data to the S4 HANA backend.

    This is how we can define the prompt and API calls to accomplish the aforementioned goals.

    • Define AI's role.
    • Transfer historical data to AI from S4, the S4 destination is used.
    • Direct API for data analysis, user feedback, and suggestion generation.
    • After the user confirms the input data, generate the payload.

    Below are the several types of texts and how the prompt is designed.

    Prompt Section

    Source

    Static prompt

    Separate HANA Db table 1- row 1

    Historic Data from SAP (backend)

    To be fetched from SAP

    First, you must retrieve the prompt pre-data from the database table. Next, you must use the second connection to retrieve the historic data from S4 and concatenate it all into a single string. In order for the AI to provide the payload in the proper format, you must also provide a sample output of the payload. Here is an example of a prompt.

    Please refer below links for best practices on prompt engineering.

    • OpenAI - https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering
    • Google - https://developers.google.com/machine-learning/resources/prompt-eng

     

  7. Things to be considered during the build phase
    1. AI Core Connection within the application. Below are the steps to enable the AI Core in the Cap application and to make it available for usage.
      1. In Package.json, use CAP LLM plugin for connecting with AI ("cap-llm-plugin": "^1.3.1",
      2. Store environment variables in. cdsrc.json and connect to the AI destination created in step 7.a.
      3. Use the remaining part of deployment URL (post .com from step 6.d) in the CHAT_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_URL variable.
      4. Also add below environment variables
        1. Resource group
        2. API version.
        3. "CHAT_MODEL_RESOURCE_GROUP": "default",
        4. "CHAT_MODEL_API_VERSION": "2023-03-15-preview", Now we are all set and can access the AI Core from your Cap application
    2. Make calls to AI Core from CAP application (to get data, to post data into genAI, get data back from genAI). To call the AI core API, please follow below steps.
      1. Connect to “cap-llm-plugin”.
      2. Use methods from the llm plugin. We had used getChatCompletion method to post data into the AI core.
      3. The inputs provided by user needs to be concatenated into the chat history and to be stored in a HANA table.
      4. The chat history + new message will go into the llm plugin.
      5. The process will continue in a loop until the AI returns the final payload which needs to be used by the application to call the final S4 API to post data.
    3. Below diagram shows how the data is passed between the different components on program execution. With these steps and guidance mentioned below, you should be able to develop an AI enabled digital assistant which can process business transactions.:
      1. Accepts user input into the application.
      2. Store the user prompt/input into the chat history DB.
      3. Get the recent prompt + historical data from table.
      4. Pass this to the LLM Core.
      5. Get the output from LLM Core.
      6. Update the response into chat history DB.
      7. Display the LLM response to user.

         

  8. Additional Information

    Pricing

    While we need to choose the AI model to be used in our application, the cost of each model varies. Details on the costing details are available in the discovery center. https://ai-core-calculator.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/uimodule/index.html#/gen The estimate is calculated based on the AI tokens that we consume. You can assume that one token = ~4 characters.

    Use Case Identification

    While we had used to used AI to create a digital assistant, you can use the service for any other requirements you may have. Look at scenarios where there is natural language processing needed, summarizations needed, insights needed etc.

entry.title: My Home in SAP S/4HANA - A Deep Dive as of 2408
entry.content:
My Home is your personalized entry page within SAP S/4HANA,  that provides you with easy access to the business insights, tasks, and applications you need to do your job with greater speed and agility. My Home lets you access and manage your to-dos, your favorite pages, your favorite apps, and your insights on any device. It builds on the functionality of the SAP Fiori launchpad and leverages Spaces and Pages (as a prerequisite). The new My Home was first released with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2208 release. Since its first release, there have been great improvements in its functionality and user experience. In this blog, we’ll revisit the features of My Home that will enhance your user experience and productivity.  

Video: Welcome to the My Home in SAP S/4HANA 

My Home in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

 

 

My Home Sections 

 

The My Home entry page is divided into different sections. You can personalize the content of each section to fit your specific needs. 

 

To-Dos 

The To-Dos section provides an overview of your daily tasks. It combines your tasks from the My Inbox application and your situations from the standard and extended My Situations applications. The tasks display relevant business information about your assigned workflow items. It enables you to process your tasks without ever having to leave My Home. If Collaborative ERP is enabled on the system, you can collaborate directly with colleagues via MS Teams to resolve issues about your tasks quickly.  

Video: Managing the To-Dos Section 

SAP S/4HANA My Home To Do Section

 

 

 

News 

The News section keeps you updated on your company's latest news, or SAP S/4HANA system news. If SAP S/4HANA system news is displaying, you can shuffle through system news from different lines of business. You can manage SAP S/4HANA system news by choosing a line of business  that interests you to stay focused on relevant information. 

Video: Personalizing the News section 

SAP S/4HANA My Home News Section

 

 

 SAP S/4HANA My Home System News articles

 

 

 

Pages  

The Pages section provides you with quick access to your favorite or most used pages. You can manage the visibility of your pages and order them in a way that enhances your productivity.  

Video: Personalizing the Pages section 

SAP S/4HANA My Home Pages Section

 

 

 

Apps 

The Apps section provides easy access to your favorite, most used, recently used, and recommended applications. The Apps section improves the discoverability of your applications. The Recommended tab offers app suggestions using SAP Business AI, based on your user-specific role assignment. You can add apps to the Favorites tab from your most used, recently used, and recommended applications. You can organize and categorize your favorite applications by using the grouping, ordering and color-coding functionality so you can access them even more efficiently. 

Video: Personalizing the Apps section 

 SAP S/4HANA My Home Apps Section

 

 

 

Insights Tiles and Insights Cards 

The Insights section allows you to manage and monitor your company’s activities. It shows critical business information through KPIs and micro charts. This section is broken into two sections: Insights Tiles and Insights Cards. Insights cards and tiles display dynamic content at a glance. They provide quick and easy access to the applications that can provide more detailed information. Insights tiles offer analytical information, such as charts or KPIs. Insights cards are similar to Insights tiles but provide more detailed analytical information in the form of charts, tables or lists from supporting overview page-, list report-, and analytical list page-based applications. Thanks to this information, you can assess what is happening at any time and take action when needed.  You can customize the Insights section to display information that enables you to do your job with speed and agility. 

Video: Personalizing the Insights Tiles Section 

Video: Personalizing the Insights Cards Section 

 SAP S/4HANA My Home Insights Section

 

 

The My Home Layout and Advanced Settings 

 You can personalize the individual My Home sections, and another key feature is the ability to customize the layout of these sections on My Home. This personalization capability allows you to prioritize the information that is most important to you. You can manage the visibility and the display order of each of the My Home sections. 

Video: Personalizing the Layout of My Home  

Once you have personalized the My Home sections you can easily share your personalized content with co-workers who have the same or a similar role as you. This  enables them to also work with speed and agility. 

Video: Sharing the Personalized Content 

 

 

 

Now that we have given you a recap of the available My Home functionality in SAP S/4HANA, we hope you enjoy working with My Home in SAP S/4HANA and reap its benefits. You can  look forward to more enhancements that will improve your My Home experience even further in future releases of SAP S/4HANA.  

Note: Innovations appear first in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. The demos in the videos were recorded on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in September 2024. 

Stay tuned! 

Caroline Welsh and Anna Freitag 

Product Managers of My Home, Cloud ERP UX Engineering.

entry.title: Enhancing the Standard Part 4: Using unsupported pricing fields in service orders
entry.content:
In my previous blog about pricing in S/4HANA Cloud for Service, I provided an overview of the concepts and configuration steps involved. In this short follow-up blog on the topic, I would like to cover a particular aspect within pricing in service transactions, that might not be widely known within the S/4HANA Cloud for Service community. This pertains to the fact that for service transactions, not all the fields that are available in the pricing field catalog can be used in an access sequence to determine pricing. Pricing in Sales transactions supports a large number of fields - for service transactions, only a subset of these fields are relevant for pricing, which can be found in the SAP Note (2808181 - Supported Price-Relevant Fields for Service Transactions) available. Due to this, even if you setup all the pricing relevant configurations using one of these fields, upon runtime in the service order, the condition record will not be set although it exists:

 

In this blog, I would like to demonstrate a practical example of what this means and a workaround of how to still make use of unsupported fields in access sequences for service transactions. In one of the customer projects for example, the business requirement was to have two surcharge condition types in the service order based on the values of the field “Product Group 1” in the product master data. This cluster of fields is an example for those not supported as part of the SAP Note mentioned above.

The workaround approach used to still achieve this business requirement is the following. The fundamental logic of the workaround is to use a custom field (on document level) instead of the unsupported standard field to build the access sequence in the service transaction.

  1. Create a custom field (Business Context: Service Document Item) named something like 'Product Group 1 SRV'. As this custom field will only be used to build the access sequence in the background, there is no need to enable this custom field in the service order UI.
  2. Create a custom logic for the enhancement point 'CRMS4_SERV_CUST_I_MODIFY'. Here, we will have a logic, where we access the value of the 'Product Group 1' field from the product master data and write this into the custom field 'Product Group 1 SRV'. The custom logic code is as follows: 

 

 

SELECT single FirstSalesSpecProductGroup
  FROM I_ProductSalesDelivery
  INTO (product_group_1)
  where Product = @SERVICEITEM-ORIGINALLYREQUESTEDPRODUCT.

SERVICEITEM_EXTENSION_OUT-YY1_Product_Group_1_SRV = product_group_1.​

 

 

  • In pricing configuration, create the required condition table and access sequence with an access that uses the service order item custom field as an input parameter. After this create the necessary price condition record.
  • With this combination of the custom field and custom logic, in the service order, the value of the product master data field 'Product Group 1' will be fetched into the service order item custom field, which will then successfully match the access sequence and condition record setup in the previous step.

With this workaround, business requirements requiring access sequences based on standard fields that are unsupported for service transactions can still be achieved to determine the desired pricing.

entry.title: SAP runs SAP: Insights into Financial Closing Transformation with SAP AFC
entry.content:
Financial Closing Transformation

As financial operations grow in complexity, organizations increasingly seek to optimize their financial closing processes. SAP Advanced Financial Closing (AFC) has emerged as a robust solution to enhance these operations, bringing automation, streamlined task management, and greater transparency to the closing cycle. This blog delves into our transformation journey through SAP AFC, exploring key steps from initial template cleanup to advanced automation and integration efforts. The outcomes have not only improved efficiency but also empowered teams to focus on more strategic, value-adding activities.

FCC Template Cleanup: A Strategic Preparation for AFC Implementation

A crucial early step in our SAP AFC implementation journey was cleaning up templates of the predecessor tool – FCC (Financial Closing Cockpit), an often overlooked but vital task. Over time, tasks were added to the FCC, many of which became obsolete due to evolving processes, yet they remained active in the system. As a result, certain teams either never used these tasks, or they were inconsistently activated, causing confusion over the true status of the financial closing.

This overload made it difficult for stakeholders to get a clear view of progress. Therefore, our initial focus was a thorough review of over 20,000 tasks in the FCC. Each unique task was assessed for its relevance, current use, and necessity. This meticulous review allowed us to filter out redundant tasks and focus on the critical ones, eliminating unnecessary clutter in the system and enhancing the overall closing process.

Following the cleanup, we identified opportunities to replace or improve outdated automations. In many instances, tasks previously performed manually were transformed into fully automated processes. This initial streamlining laid the groundwork for more sophisticated automations and faster financial closing cycles, ensuring that only essential tasks entered the AFC framework.

Replacing Third-Party Automations with SAP iRPA: Efficiency and Cost Reduction

One of the significant challenges in modern financial departments is managing diverse automation tools. Before implementing AFC, we relied on third-party automation platforms, which, while functional, came with high operational costs and limited scalability. SAP’s native automation tool, SAP SBPA (SAP Build Process Automation, previously SAP intelligent Robotics Process Automation), proved to be a game-changer, offering deep integration with SAP environments, and reducing external dependencies.

In a relatively short period, we developed, tested, and deployed multiple SAP iRPA bots dedicated to various financial processes. These bots now handle essential tasks such as bank reconciliation preparation and the analysis and posting of general bad debts.

We currently operate eight unattended bots in SAP iRPA, each designed to execute specific financial operations autonomously. These bots have streamlined processes, eliminated manual intervention, and substantially reduced the time required to complete routine tasks. The shift from third-party to SAP automation not only simplified our tech stack but also led to measurable cost reductions by consolidating our tools within the SAP ecosystem.

Simplifying Authorization Management and SCIM Interface Integration

Effective role and authorization management is vital for maintaining security and operational efficiency, especially in complex financial environments. Prior to implementing SAP AFC, our authorization management processes, while functional, required streamlining to improve efficiency and ease of use. Roles were often unclear, and responsibilities were scattered across various departments, leading to inefficiencies in task execution and oversight.

The integration of the SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) interface into SAP AFC introduced a new level of simplicity and precision to our authorization processes. This integration allowed for the automatic provisioning and de-provisioning of users based on their roles, making it easier to manage user access and control.

The new role structure under AFC is much clearer and more effective, aligning specific tasks with precise static and instance authorizations such as end-user, support, or administrator. Additionally, scoped roles further refine access by limiting user permissions to specific areas, such as country-specific operations or department-specific tasks. This ensures that users only have access to what is necessary for their roles, reducing the risk of unauthorized access. This shift not only improved operational clarity but also enhanced security by reducing the risk of unauthorized access. The streamlined approach to authorization management also facilitates quicker onboarding for new team members and minimizes downtime related to role changes.

Task Structure and Management Optimization: Building for Efficiency

One of the standout features of SAP AFC is the ability to create Task List Models in the backend, which simplifies the administration and management of financial closing tasks. This capability allowed us to develop a new structure for task management, linking closing tasks to our financial closing timetable. The timetable is accessible as a high-level overview within AFC, giving managers and stakeholders an intuitive view of the entire closing process.

As a result, task management has become significantly more organized and accessible. This task management system not only streamlines task administration but also ensures logical connections between tasks, making them easier to track. Through the "Manage Closing Task Lists" app, authorized users can oversee the closing structure, manage dependencies, and task parameters, and effortlessly generate new task lists.

Monitoring task execution times and error rates has been another critical aspect of this transformation. By analysing this data, we can make real-time adjustments to the process, optimizing execution times and improving user experiences. This continuous improvement cycle has helped us minimize delays, address bottlenecks promptly, and ensure that our financial closing runs smoothly.

Expanding Automation: Continuous Collaboration and Innovation

Automation has been central to our AFC transformation, but our efforts didn’t stop at standard automations. By collaborating with different finance teams and other departments, we continuously identify new use cases for automation. This collaborative approach has allowed us to expand the scope of AFC beyond its original framework.

One of the key benefits of AFC is its flexibility. We’ve used this to our advantage by enabling teams to easily review the results of automated tasks and monitor their progress. Rather than introducing new manual tasks, we have focused on extending automation to cover more scenarios. This has been particularly useful for high-volume, repetitive tasks, where automation reduces the risk of human error and accelerates completion.

A key feature that has facilitated this expansion is the use of task dependencies, which allow for the automatic triggering of subsequent tasks once preceding tasks are completed. This has enabled us to create process chains that execute with minimal intervention, further reducing the burden on our teams.

Better Task Organization and Responsibility Distribution Across Finance Departments

A key improvement in our financial closing processes has been the enhanced organization and clarity of tasks. By adopting a standardized naming convention and utilizing custom fields, we can now easily identify which financial area each task pertains to. This improved organization has simplified task management across the department, making it easier to assign and track responsibilities.

In the past, hundreds of users were involved in triggering and monitoring individual tasks, which required significant coordination. With AFC, we have shifted to a more centralized approach, where a smaller group of users is responsible for specific tasks. By using user groups, we have reduced the need for widespread user involvement, freeing up valuable resources to focus on higher-value activities. This simplification has been a game-changer, allowing us to concentrate on more strategic tasks rather than being bogged down by the execution of repetitive, low-value processes.

Significant Error Reduction and Faster Closing Cycle

One of the most significant results of our AFC implementation has been the dramatic reduction in error rates. By reviewing each task and continuously developing improvements, we’ve been able to eliminate many common issues that previously plagued the financial closing process.

Today, manual tasks are primarily reserved for documentation, approval, or compliance-related checks, with most tasks now fully automated. This shift has not only improved accuracy but also shortened the overall financial closing cycle.

A key feature supporting this transformation is the Financial Closing Overview and Closing Task completion apps within AFC, which provides real-time insights into the status of the closing process. Users can monitor both the general progress and individual tasks with ease, allowing them to make timely decisions and adjustments. Proper notification scenarios ensure that users are always informed about task status, further enhancing the efficiency of the closing process. The current automation rate in AFC-integrated systems is around 88%, indicating high operational efficiency. Additionally, the error rate for automated tasks has been significantly reduced and stabilized at approximately 5%, well within the acceptable failure criteria for optimal performance.

The Future of AFC: Expanding Automation with Group Reporting Integration and AI-Guided Error Resolution

Looking ahead, we are planning to integrate Group Reporting with AFC, marking a new chapter in our financial closing journey. This integration will bring even greater transparency and efficiency to our financial reporting processes, allowing us to further extend the scope of automation and process optimization. In addition to this, we are currently testing and improving our new solution for Gen AI-guided error resolution in AFC, which promises to significantly enhance the speed and accuracy of identifying and resolving issues.

In conclusion, SAP AFC has been instrumental in transforming our financial closing processes. By reducing manual tasks, enhancing automation, simplifying task management, and improving user experiences, we’ve not only streamlined operations but also positioned our teams to focus on strategic, value-adding activities. Our AFC journey is ongoing, and as we continue to innovate and expand its scope, including advancements in AI-driven solutions, we look forward to achieving even greater operational excellence in the future.

entry.title: Integration & Exception Monitoring Two-Tier ERP
entry.content:
Introduction

Integration and exception monitoring in a Two-Tier ERP system are essential to ensure seamless data flow and consistency between Headquarter and Subsidiary systems. They help maintain operational efficiency, avoid data discrepancies, and support accurate reporting across business units. Real-time exception monitoring enables quick identification and resolution of issues, preventing disruptions. Proper monitoring ensures that any deviations are swiftly addressed, ensuring smooth, unified business operations

Set up:

Let see a detailed view on how we can set up Integration & Exception Monitoring for SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Public cloud Edition and CI.

The details are also available in reference Link.

Process

Let’s deep dive into the process looking at the use case.

Case 1:  In 2EL scenario  we send the sales order to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition  from On-premise(SAP S/4HANA) system as Headquarter wants to purchase from Subsidiary.

In this case the communication is failing at Headquarter.

Case 2:  In 2EL scenario we send the sales order to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition  from On-premise system(SAP S/4HANA) as Headquarter wants to purchase from Subsidiary.

In this case the communication is failing at CI which is a middleware.

References

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/setup-administration/landscape-setup

https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal/setup-managed-services.html

https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations.html?anchorId=section_1159650512

entry.title: Flexible Item Category Determination in SAP Cloud Public - Custom Adaptions
entry.content:
Why Flexible Item Category Determination Matters!

As Sales Configuration Experts, we all know that the Item categories control how sales document items behave and thus meet different demands in the business processes.

However, the limitations of the Sales Public Cloud prevent the creation of Z-Sales Document Types. As a result, we are unable to control the determination of the appropriate item category that will control the item behavior in the sales documents. Consequently, most users are required to make manual changes when processing these documents.

Flexible Determination

Using the Flexible Item Category Determination, The system can evaluate specific business conditions and propose an item category according to your business rules instead of the Default Item Category assignment.

Example:

Your client needs to manage the behavior of business processes by selecting the appropriate Order Reason. For a certain type of orders, a down payment is required for all products. Therefore, the client will select "Down Payment" from the dropdown list. Once this selection is made, the system can propose the CBAO item category when specific business conditions outlined in a decision table are met, allowing the billing plan to be automatically enabled.

Define the following Business Rule to Your Decision Table.

 

Sales Document Type Item Category Group Item Category Usage Item Category of Higher-Level Item SD Order Reason Item Category

 

= OR= NORM<empty><empty>= 01 (Down Payment)= CBAO

Note: If the user doesn't select any order reason or any available alternatives, the system finds no logic in your decision table. The system proposes the default item category TAN.

Step-by-Step Guide to Configuration

Step 1: Create PRFplus Objects

you can create Business Rule Framework Plus (BRFplus) applications, functions, and expressions from templates.

You perform this in the configuration activity Create BRFplus Objects for Sales Document Categories.

  • Custom Adaption: ITEM_CATEGORY_DETERMINATITEION

 

  • Select Document Category: C (Orders) And Press Enter

     

A BRFplus application template is provided. The application template includes a function template and an expression template.

 

  • Press The Copy Button to Enter the PRFplus Object (PRFplus Application)  

     

     

  • Save Your Configuration
  • Navigate Simplified BRFplus Workbench app and Configure your Business Rules.

     

  • In the following Application you can define the new Business Rules to your Decision Table.
    • Find your application in the repository.
    • Expand the application node, the expression node, and then the decision table node.
    • Choose the decision table to display it.
    • Click Edit

 

 

 

  • Now you can add a row to your decision table and add values in cells as shown below.

     

  • You can now check, save and activate Your Business Rules.
     
  • Recommendation

    To prevent any inconsistencies between your business rules and the existing item category assignments in the Assign Item Categories configuration activity, choose values for the following conditions and the result by using the search help:

    • Sales document type
    • Item category group
    • Item category of higher-level item
    • Item usage
    • Item category

     Results

    The implementation is complete for the sales document category to which the activated BRFplus application, function, and expression are assigned. The flexible item category determination is enabled for sales documents that belong to the sales document category. 

Testing the Configuration

  1. Create Standard Sales Order And Choose the Order Reason defined in your Business Rule.

 

Thanks!

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Central Business Configuration 2409 FD2
entry.content:
With the latest SAP Central Business Configuration 2409 FD2 release, an exciting enhancement is now available. This blog post will provide you with a detailed overview of this enhancement.

Enhanced Notifications

Notifications in Project Experience are prominently displayed and easily accessible via the bell icon, ensuring users stay informed and up to date. The enhanced notifications are now displayed for all projects assigned to a user in a structured format. This eliminates the need to switch between projects to view project-specific notifications.

The notifications will remain visible until they are dismissed. Users can conveniently view notifications organized by project, notification type, or date. Additionally, users with the SAP_CBC_CONSUMPTION_PROGRAM_LEAD role can access notifications for all projects.

Note: Please be informed that the notifications are visible only if the email address is maintained for the business users.

Bell Icon with notifications

 For a quick summary, check out this video about  What's New in 2409 FD2. Don’t forget to also use the What’s New Viewer on the SAP Help Portal, which provides further details and links about all the recent changes.

That wraps up the new features this time. Make sure to follow the SAP Central Business Configuration tag or follow this dedicated blog collection to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

Do you have an idea on how to further enhance SAP Central Business Configuration? Go to the SAP Customer Influence portal to submit an improvement request or vote for the ideas of others. This helps us understand your requirements even better and prioritize our work accordingly. We’re looking forward to your improvement requests.
